title: Elasticsearch ILM Policy Failures
spl:
index=database sourcetype="elasticsearch:ilm_status"
| where step="ERROR" OR action_time_millis > 3600000
| stats count as error_count, latest(failed_step) as failed_step, latest(step_info) as reason by index, policy
| sort -error_count
description: Failed ILM transitions leave indices in the wrong lifecycle phase â€” hot data stays on expensive storage, old data never deletes, rollover stops creating new indices. Silent failures accumulate until disk fills.
value: Failed ILM transitions leave indices in the wrong lifecycle phase â€” hot data stays on expensive storage, old data never deletes, rollover stops creating new indices. Silent failures accumulate until disk fills.
implementation: Poll `GET */_ilm/explain` periodically and extract indices where `step` equals `ERROR`. Capture the `failed_step`, `step_info`, and `phase_time` for root cause analysis. Alert on any index stuck in ERROR. Also monitor indices that have been in the same phase longer than expected (e.g., hot phase > 30 days when policy says 7 days).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
â€˘ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Custom REST scripted input (`_ilm/explain`).
â€˘ Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=elasticsearch:ilm_status`.
â€˘ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 â€” Configure data collection
Poll `GET */_ilm/explain` periodically and extract indices where `step` equals `ERROR`. Capture the `failed_step`, `step_info`, and `phase_time` for root cause analysis. Alert on any index stuck in ERROR. Also monitor indices that have been in the same phase longer than expected (e.g., hot phase > 30 days when policy says 7 days).

Step 2 â€” Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=database sourcetype="elasticsearch:ilm_status"
| where step="ERROR" OR action_time_millis > 3600000
| stats count as error_count, latest(failed_step) as failed_step, latest(step_info) as reason by index, policy
| sort -error_count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Elasticsearch ILM Policy Failures** â€” Failed ILM transitions leave indices in the wrong lifecycle phase â€” hot data stays on expensive storage, old data never deletes, rollover stops creating new indices. Silent failures accumulate until disk fills.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=elasticsearch:ilm_status`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Custom REST scripted input (`_ilm/explain`). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedâ€”rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: database; **sourcetype**: elasticsearch:ilm_status. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

â€˘ Scopes the data: index=database, sourcetype="elasticsearch:ilm_status". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
â€˘ Filters the current rows with `where step="ERROR" OR action_time_millis > 3600000` â€” typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
â€˘ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by index, policy** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
â€˘ Orders rows with `sort` â€” combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 â€” Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 â€” Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (indices in error with reason), Single value (error count), Bar chart (errors by policy).
visualization: Table (indices in error with reason), Single value (error count), Bar chart (errors by policy).
